speaker: interviewer
text: July the 31, 1995. The survivor is Marcia Ceitlin-Spies. Joyce Tapper, interviewer. Encino, USA, to be in English.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is July 31, 1995. Our survivor is Marcia Spies. My name is Joyce Tapper. I'm the interview. We're interviewing in English. And we're in Encino, California. [INAUDIBLE] like your kids.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: All right, I am Marcia Ceitlin is-- was my maiden name, and Spies is my married name. And I was born in Kovno, Lithuania in 1929, December 13, 1929. My parents' names were Genia. My mother was Genia. Taft was her maiden name, and Ceitlin. And my father was Mayer Zvi Ceitlin. My father was born in [INAUDIBLE], and he came to Kovno. Actually, [INAUDIBLE] is a suburb of Kovno. It's about five minutes from Kovno. Kovno used to be the capital of Lithuania then. And he met my mother and they were married in Kovno [INAUDIBLE]. I think it was 1926 that they were married. My mother was a very-- both of my parents, actually, were very, very ambitious people and my mother already was in business at the age of probably 20. She was like 25, 26 at the time. And my father came, and-- and they met.

speaker: interviewer
text: What sort of business was she in?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was always in clothing and fabric business. And when my father came, he had a-- he was an accountant, and they married, and-- and they immediately moved the business into a larger one. And my father was really very involved in-- in larger-- he was better in wholesale and [? overall ?] and my mother was much better in retail. So it was a real good combination. And they just-- in no time, they just grew and their business grew and grew. And by the time I was born, 1929, I think it was about a year later, we moved into a beautiful home. And I--

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I can describe my home. My home was really very modern, I'm thinking. Because I was just back in Lithuania two years ago. First time I was back was six years ago, and then second time was two years ago. And when I went up, my house is still standing there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's the address?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was 19-- no, it was 40 [INAUDIBLE] in [INAUDIBLE], Lithuania. And our store was on the same property as our home. We lived, actually, in 1940-- up until 1940, we lived in two homes, which were right there because when I-- in 1930, when my parents moved to that larger home, we lived there for about six years and then we moved to another home which they built, a second story on top of the store.

speaker: interviewee
text: So when I went back, I realized how very, very modern it was. We had parquet floors. We had, of course, several bathrooms. We had beautiful balconies. Well, we were really considered [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] well-to-do people, and I guess that's one of the reasons why the Russians sent out. So we had governesses. And I had a governess. And my little sister had another governess. We had cooks and seamstresses who came to, you know, sew for us. And I was really spoiled, I must say. But that was the way the parents thought that they, you know, would bring up their children and everything, so there wouldn't be anything missing. What was that Jewish community like for your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: All right, my father was a very religious man. He belonged to the Lubavitcher. He was a Lubavitcher Hasid. And the rebbe was really the ultimate. He would never do anything without consulting the rebbe. I have very fond memories of that movement because that was always a very happy time in our lives, in our home. Holidays was always very gay with music and [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] songs. And-- and my father, being a religious man, was very, very busy, as I remember, in his business. But came Friday night, I realized right away was Shabbos. Everything would go from his pockets. I remember it to this day, in a handkerchief, and he-- he would tie it, first one-- one end-- first tie it in one end and then the other end, and it would go into a disc, into a buffet. And there was a key there. And-- and right away he would start clapping with his hands. And we kids would be flying-- he would lift us up to the ceiling, and he would kiss us and it was like Shabbos Queen came to our home, like really truly like they say the Sabbath Queen descends on the homes. My father built a synagogue right next to our house. It was a very large synagogue. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: You said your father built it. Explain that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I mean he contributed the money to build the synagogue. I suppose it was with the help of other people, but it was right-- there was a fence right next to our-- between our house and the synagogue. And so, of course, Friday night there was always going to the synagogue. It was an Orthodox synagogue. The women were upstairs, the men were downstairs. The-- and every Friday night we would have a guest come to her house for dinner. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: The-- my father had-- my grandmother lived with us until she died in 1939. And she was-- pardon me? What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name was Kaila Mary, and she was truly the queen of the house. She was very revered and my parents brought-- brought us up-- excuse me-- with the idea that elder people have to be respected.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this your mother's mother or your father's mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it was my father's mother. And she-- my grandfather had died, and so she came to live with us right after her grand-- my grandfather died. And she was Babushka, we used to call her, which is grandmother in Russian. And so there wasn't morning when we wouldn't-- before going to school we would go over to her headquarters, where she lived. She had her own bedroom and bathroom, a whole wing. And we would kiss her hello, good morning, and the evening before we went to bed. [BACKGROUND NOISE] We would-- yeah, excuse me. We would immediately, before we went to bed, we would again go over and kiss her and say goodbye, you know, good night. Now, my grandmother lived with us until she died 1939. My parents were always respectful of her. And she-- they always-- I never heard any cross words, you know, any-- any time, saying anything bad about my grandmother or anything criticizing her. She was always, whatever Grandmother said, you know, we did it. In the home my father had three-- three sisters. And so the sisters would come to our house. It was a gathering place for everybody because my grandmother was there and, of course, my father was the only son as well. So I think a long time ago there was-- my father had a brother, but he died and no one ever spoke about him. I think he died-- not quite sure of what he died of. But it was some secret of disease or mental disease. I'm not quite sure what it was. Now on my mother's side, my grandmother-- and my grandmother lived not far from us. They had a large bakery and so they were also very well-to-do. I remember my other grandmother quite well because I would stop by there and get some-- get some cake and so on. And I was named after my grandfather, my mother's father, Mathis. So my Hebrew name was really [INAUDIBLE], something like it. But they called me Marcia. We didn't have any middle names in those days. My mother also had a brother. He was a doctor-- [? Labat ?] Taft. And then he had another-- she had another brother, Gershon Taft. He was in charge of the bakery when my grandfather died. And two of my aunts on my father's side lived in Kovno. One-- one-- one sister's name was Paula, and she was quite significant in my life.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what way?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, later on I will tell you that when-- she really-- I lived with them, with my Aunt Paula and Aunt Chassia. And Aunt Chassia passed away in Stutthof. Now, my-- on my father's side-- and then I had another aunt who was in Paris. And she was Assia. Her name was Assia. And I did, when I came to the United States, I lived with her, and she really was the one who brought me out here. And she was really like a second mother to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now my-- my father, as I mentioned-- my mother, as I mentioned, had two brothers. And, well, what else can I tell you? We were, as I said, we-- we were very well off. And so we had-- What was your school experience like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I went to Hebrew school and [INAUDIBLE] Gymnasium. First I went to [INAUDIBLE], I think, for a year, and then [INAUDIBLE] Gymnasium. And until 1940. In 1940, the Russians came to Lithuania, and we were forbidden to go to Hebrew schools. So it was-- I believe it was partially Yiddish and partially Russian. And until 1941. And in 1941, the Germans came in.

speaker: interviewer
text: Prior to that, did you encounter any anti-Semitic incidents?

speaker: interviewee
text: I personally did not encounter any. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Of course, I was only 11, and we were really living in a quite, I would say-- even though we had the stores and my-- my parents had lots of people, friends, Lithuanians, and Polish descent, and Russian descent. But I-- I-- I never knew anything about anti-Semitism. But, as I said, I was more or less going to Hebrew school so I was in a Jewish environment.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was--

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- in-- in the school-- excuse me-- in the schools, we-- Hebrew was the main language. At home, we spoke Yiddish. Since Lithuania was a Russian-- Russia [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] was a Russian satellite, Russian was very prevalent in-- as a language in Lithuania. And of course, Lithuanian was, as well. Lithuanian language is quite different from Russian. It's really a Baltic language. Lithuanian and Latvian are similar, but not Estonian.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so, as a child, of course, at the age, right away, when I started to speak, I learned Yiddish, which we spoke at home, and Russian. I had a Russian governess, so I learned Russian. And I learned German. I had a German governess. And Lithuanian, of course, I had to learn from being in Lithuania. And, let's see what else? What-- well and Hebrew. Then I went to Hebrew school so I learned some Hebrew. So we really sort of spoke interchangeably different languages. What was your first sign that something was going wrong and that there was danger?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in 1940, the Russians came in. And the-- they-- our store was confiscated. And we were driven out of our-- of our second home.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they confiscate your store? What happened? Do you remember the day?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well-- the Russians, I believe, came in about June 1940. And I think it might have been a few months earlier. And I remember that right away, it was an upheaval in-- in-- in our home. And shortly, within a month, people were evicted from their homes, from their stores. So it was nationalized. They brought in-- the Russians just drove my parents out of the store and brought in their own people. And it became a government store. They just-- they just nationalized the stores, all the wealth from the different people. And we were permitted to live in one of our houses, mainly because my father knew the so-called lay of the land, all the-- the upkeep, where the different heaters were, the water heaters, and various things. So they permitted him to stay as a janitor. And I can even remember in the winter time him having to go out and-- and shovel the snow, and-- and sweep the ground. But we were-- and then pretty soon, everything, they used to come into the home and just usurp various goods of-- of-- of value. And so I remember my mother putting-- we had a large grand piano, black piano, and she placed it with some Polish people, Polish descent, who lived nearby. And every day I would go-- well, maybe three, four times a week-- I would go and play the piano, practice. And so that piano really played a significant part in my life because it saved my life, I think. And, oh, various things I remember that they buried money and converted a lot of the valuables into gold coins and put them in bottles and buried them under the ground-- underground under our house. And again, I, later on, when-- in our store-- and our house was converted into a headquarters for the secret police.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this happened already in 1941-- '40-- yeah, in 1941, perhaps. OK, now let me see. How were you feeling? How-- what-- how did all this affect you as a small child, a medium child at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Well, I was always very self-sufficient, really born to be a survivor. And I-- I knew, I thought I knew what I wanted, you know, and I wanted independence. I wanted to do my own thinking and do my own thing. And-- and I was really very stubborn. And I remember that my parents would say that they went through all the governesses-- in Lithuania they had to import them because I just didn't want anybody to follow me in school and everywhere I went, they would have to follow me and see what I'm doing and whom I'm playing with, and so I'd make their life so miserable that they would-- that they would leave. And they'd have to bring other ones in from all around.

speaker: interviewee
text: So how it affected me, well, at first, I really thought, since I always dreamt of being independent as a child, and I'd say why do I need somebody to follow me and tell me what to do? So I would say, one day, I'm going to run away from-- from home, and-- or I wish I didn't have anybody, any parents, to-- to tell me what to do and how to do. So when the war started, I really-- or-- well, the-- when the Germans came in, I went to a camp. Just before the Germans came, I went to a summer camp. And, again, against my parents' wishes, because they knew that-- that our-- they-- they used to take the wealthy people, send them out to Siberia to get rid of them.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, word went around that this will happen, that they would be sent out. So my parents didn't want us to-- summertime was just when school was over. But I was very insistent on going to summer camp, so they finally consented to let me go for two weeks. And I left on a Sunday, and on a Tuesday, the-- they were sent out to Siberia, the whole family-- my mother and father and two sisters. And they uncovered there, the secret police came in at night and lined up my parents against the wall and told my mother to take whatever she wanted, that they were just-- they were just moving them to another-- to another country-- to another city. And so I was away in camp in Palanga, which was a-- by the seashore.

speaker: interviewer
text: How far away was it from Kovno?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say it was about 30 miles from Kovno. So I didn't know anything about it. And-- so this was Tuesday night, and they were placed in-- in animal wagons, cars, trains. And they were there at the station, from what I understand, for about two days. My father, in turn, was separated from my mother and two sisters. And my mother was-- both of my parents were really remarkable, but especially my mother because you can imagine the feelings she had. I mean here she had a two-year-old and a seven-year-old with her. One daughter, a 10-year-old or 11-year-old, was away in the summer camp. My father was in another cart. And they were just treated terribly without food, without any water. I remember, my aunt-- but they were permitted to have the family come. So my my-- and my Aunt Paula and Chassia, my-- my father's sisters, would come-- would come there and, you know, bring them food, and water, and so on. And told them not to worry, that they will take care of me when I come back.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they were actually sent out on a Thursday, and on a Friday or Saturday, the Germans marched in. Now, when the Germans marched into Lithuania, I was in summer camp. And-- What was the summer camp about? Was it a-- describe it.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a Zionist camp. I believe it was Habonim, and we were all from school. We belonged to this group of-- Zionist group. And so they had a summer camp and that's where I went. So, immediately, when the Germans came in, and I remember them coming in, they opened up the camp and, you know, started to-- were thinking of annihilating Jewish-- I mean, Jewish children, all in-- that's the last thing they ever needed.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you see that day?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, what I saw was the Germans with their-- I remember, with their hats, with their hard hats over them, with-- with guns, and-- what do you call them? The-- that they beat the children-- you know, you know, that-- the-- [INAUDIBLE], whatever they-- you call them. And I remember them opening up the doors and saying, out, everybody out. And so we were permitted to just take whatever we could carry with us. And they started marching us from place to place. This went on for about, I would say, four or five days.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was summer.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in the summer and it was hot, I remember. And we marched in the middle of the street. And there were like five people in a line. And there were many children there. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they feed you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they didn't feed us. And that was really my first encounter with hunger. I remember-- I remember that I-- some of the children just couldn't-- you know, really gave up, or-- or they were complaining, or crying. But I always found a way of dealing with-- with the situation. And I'd say, well, let's look what can we do? So I remember picking up a garbage can, the cover to a garbage can, and taking it off and-- and taking out some potato peels and eating it. And, for survival. And we would pick up some grass, you know, something that looked like it was edible.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many children were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know exactly, but I would imagine there must have been about 200 children.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened to the-- your counselors, your teachers?

speaker: interviewee
text: --of all different ages. Well, they went marching with us, as I recall. At night, we would-- we would-- they would give us-- and the Germans were in front and in back of us-- and they-- the German soldiers. And they marched us to different barns. And in a barn, they would just-- we would sleep on with the-- with the animals on hay, I remember that.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I approached everything as an exciting way of-- of happening. I always looked forward to some challenges, to some excitement. And I felt that this was, well, this was great, you know? I mean, it wasn't great, but it was something that I will survive, you know, that I will went-- go through. And finally, about a week later, we started getting packages through the Red Cross. And my-- Where were you at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was still at the seashore in Palanga. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: You were marching still?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, no-- well, yes, we were marching, but finally we were, as I recall, after about four or five days, we-- some officials came and insisted that we be in a house, in some large barn or house, whatever. And the Red Cross would stop by, and I remember that very distinctly, and start calling out names for packages. And so we got packages. And I remember opening up-- in fact, it was a brown package with string around, and-- and you know, saying, from my father, there was a note there saying, you know, this is something for you. I love you, and so on, and signed my father. And there were cookies and candy. And-- and we were really just overjoyed with all this.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, after a few days, we were taken by buses, and we went back to Kovno. Well, at the station, my aunts were there to meet me. And I said, well, what happened? Where are my parents? And they said, well, why don't you come to the house and we'll tell you? So then they told me the story that my parents were sent out and that, you know. So they really forged my parents' name. They signed it. They didn't want to alarm me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, later on, I found out my Aunt Paula was really the one who was instrumental to bring all the children back from the-- from the Palanga, from the camp. If it weren't for my aunt, perhaps they might have even annihilated all of us. But she risked her life to go out and-- and round the parents up so that-- through the Red Cross, so that the children would-- should be able to come back.
